moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the King's Indian Attack, a flexible system that doesn't commit to e4 immediately but builds a solid setup with fianchetto ideas. Nf3 controls the central squares e5 and d4 while keeping our options open—we can support a later e4 push or transition into a quieter position depending on Black's response.

Black mirrors our setup, developing a knight that controls key central squares (e4 and d5) and prepares kingside castling. This is a natural, flexible response that doesn't commit Black to any particular pawn structure yet.

Here we commit to the fianchetto setup, preparing to place our bishop on g2 where it will control the long diagonal (a1-h8) and exert pressure toward d5 and e5. This is the hallmark of the King's Indian Attack—we're building a solid, space-controlling position from which we can gradually improve our pieces.

Black strikes in the center immediately, taking space and forcing us to decide how to handle the central tension. This aggressive setup is typical against the King's Indian Attack; Black wants to avoid allowing us a smooth expansion with e4.

We complete our fianchetto setup, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it bears down on d5 and controls the center from afar. With Bg2 and the coming Nf3 and O-O, we'll have a stable, harmonious position where our pieces support each other and constrain Black's central pawns.

Black supports the d5 pawn and prepares Be7, indicating a solid, classical setup. By playing e6, Black reinforces the center and keeps flexibility—the bishop can go to e7 or d6, and the e-file may become important for rook placement later.

We castle kingside, tucking our king to safety and preparing to activate our rook on e1 or f1. This move signals that our position is solid enough that we're happy to focus on piece coordination and gradual improvements rather than pursuing immediate central breakthroughs.

Black completes a compact, defensive setup, stationing the bishop on e7 where it supports d5 and prepares to connect the rooks after castling. We now have a typical King's Indian Attack position where we enjoy a space advantage and the long diagonal pressure, while Black has a solid, flexible structure that's hard to break down—our next moves should focus on improving our pieces (perhaps Nc3 and a move like c4 or Nbd2) to gradually push for an advantage.
